evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
Entity of type FAQPage has no @id — cross-page interconnection blocked
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
Specific human voice vs committee 4/10 — Mostly corporate editorial voice with occasional injected 'spicy' lines that feel pasted in.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 5/10 — A few pointed opinions surface (lab numbers, annual physicals are theatre) but most claims are hedged.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — Some vivid lines ('quietly rotting through spring') but diluted by generic SEO boilerplate.
Point of view vs survey 5/10 — Takes a mild stance but mostly surveys recommendations neutrally.
Quotable sentence 6/10 — 'Annual physicals are theatre' and 'the desert humbles every calendar' are quotable; rest is forgettable.
Intro earns attention 4/10 — The strong hook exists but is buried under TL;DR, bylines, and repeated boilerplate before the reader gets to it.

intro_hook 4/10 — The opening sentence is a generic demographic statement with zero tension or specificity. It's functionally identical to 'In today's world, many people...' The TL;DR above it is strong, but the actual prose hook is flat and earns no attention. The body later contains a genuinely compelling line ('By mid-March, I've watched half my clients quietly abandon January's goals') buried in Key Takeaways—that energy needs to open the post.

specificity 6/10 — The post has strong specificity in places (AED 1,200, March 19 date, named tests like fasting glucose and lipid panel, DHA/MOHAP named bodies). But the practical adaptation section drifts into abstraction: 'hotter months,' 'local vegetables,' 'midday jolt drinks,' 'energy challenges,' and 'current conditions' are vague placeholders. The nutrition section lists 'whole grains, legumes, and local vegetables' without naming a single item (lentils, lupin, Arabic cucumber). The behaviour-change advice uses generic wellness language — 'positive routines,' 'long-term health change' — without a concrete before/after scenario anchored in Dubai life.
